query: cancer risk in astronauts due to cosmic radiation

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Health Maintenance Wellness -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: While diet and exercise are the cornerstones of health maintenance and wellness in the hostile environment of aerospace, an important consideration is the deleterious effects of radiation. Once leaving the protective atmosphere and magnetosphere of Earth, astronauts are subjected to solar and cosmic radiation, so engineering controls to provide shielding are paramount. Solar radiation includes ultraviolet rays, x-rays, protons, and electrons. Cosmic radiation emanates from every direction in deep space and consists of high-energy protons and atomic nuclei which are far more energetic, and therefore more damaging than solar radiation. Because of current concerns of lifetime radiation exposure and resulting cancer, astronauts selected for a long duration, deep space mission will likely be older.

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Physical Effects -- Issues of Concern -- Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Humans in the aerospace environment are exposed to an increased ionizing radiation index, leading to genetic and cytogenetic changes. One concern is that an unpredicted solar particle event (eg, solar flares) may expose space crew to unacceptably high radiation levels and cause acute radiation syndrome, which typically manifests following whole-body or partial-body exposure greater than 0.5 Gy. [1] Symptoms occur as a spectrum, dependent on the type, route, and exposure dose, and typically lead to hematopoietic, gastrointestinal, neurovascular, and cutaneous manifestations. [1] [2] Of additional concern is chronic exposure to low-dose cosmic radiation. This exposure may be a factor for space crew and long-haul commercial pilots or transport aircrew who experience increased cosmic and ultraviolet radiation at the typical cruising altitudes of modern aircraft (ie, 10000 to 13000 m). Chronic exposure has been shown to increase the risk of nuclear cataracts in pilots compared with non-pilots. [3] Although anecdotal evidence suggests that aircrews are at an increased risk for malignancy, studies have reported conflicting results. Further research is warranted to ascertain how much exposure to cosmic radiation affects subsequent long-term pathology. [4] [5] [6] [7] Aircrew and space crew may also sustain increased radiation exposure from vehicle systems (eg, radar systems) specific to the aircraft. However, there is only limited research regarding this factor. Humans in space are unavoidably exposed to increased amounts of cosmic radiation, and spacecraft employ radiation protection material to mitigate this risk. Additionally, limits to cumulative radiation exposure are enforced. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Health Maintenance Wellness -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, engineering controls are vital to protecting food from the adverse effects of radiation so that when consumed after long storage, whether onboard ship or pre-placed, nutrient content will be maintained and quality will be preserved.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation-Induced Coronary Artery Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation exposure can be natural or iatrogenic. Most natural exposure does not result in high radiation levels at a single time, and sources of this type of exposure include radon gas in the ground seeping into basements and crawlspaces, cosmic rays from sun exposure, and high altitude. [2] More often, patients are exposed to radiation through medical imaging or therapy, which explains the high incidence of RICAD in post-RT patients. Radiation-induced coronary artery disease is primarily caused by prior mediastinal radiation exposure for multiple cancers affecting the mediastinum, including breast cancer, Hodgkin’s lymphoma, lung cancer, esophageal cancer, and thymoma. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiology Patient Safety and Communication -- Issues of Concern -- Concepts for Enabling Effective Communication About Risk vs. Benefits with Focus on Ionizing Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: As with other risk prediction calculations involving multiple variables that independently affect the outcome, the way to estimate hypothetical risks of radiation on future cancer development for a given person is to use a mathematical model that incorporates the independent patient parameters (e.g., gender, age of exposure, dose) from previously obtained samples. Risk calculators available for public use (as mentioned below) are typically based on the BEIR VII committee's model, derived from data from the Life Span Study on the Hiroshima and Nagasaki atomic bomb survivor cohort and a control cohort of citizens living nearby in the subsequent decades. However, the BEIR VII committee also applied data from studies on medical (mostly therapeutic), occupational, and environmental radiation exposures. Models of cancer risk from one or cumulative radiological exams exemplify the principles discussed above, such as the linear no-threshold hypothesis and increased rate of adverse outcome based on the age of the patient exposed. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Exposure Of Medical Imaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is a consensus that ionizing radiation is oncogenic. Much of this agreement is based upon observation of the increased incidence of carcinoma in a population surviving a nuclear attack or in uranium miners exposed to radiation at the workplace. The amount of radiation used by imaging modalities is negligible compared to the abovementioned exposures. For instance, in the United States, people are exposed to average annual background radiation levels of about 3 mSv; exposure from a chest X-ray is about 0.1 mSv, and exposure from a whole-body computerized tomography scan is about 10 mSv, and that’s 1 of the reasons why physicians usually miscalculate the potential risks associated with the radiation exposure while performing procedures using radiologic imaging. [1] [2] This topic explains how to quantify radiation, the biological effect of radiation, risks to healthcare workers due to radiation exposure, and certain recommendations and tips for various medical professionals. Radiation is defined as a moving form of energy. It can be classified into 2 categories: ionizing and non-ionizing. Ionizing radiation can be further classified into electromagnetic radiation (matterless) and particulate radiation. See Diagram. Classification of Radiation. Electromagnetic radiations are energy packets (photons) traveling as a wave. Basic examples of electromagnetic radiation are x-rays and gamma rays. Particulate radiation consists of a beam of particles that can be either charged or neutral. Electromagnetic radiations have high energy and can easily penetrate body tissues. Ionizing radiation is mainly used for diagnostic purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Health Maintenance Wellness -- Issues of Concern -- Gemini
retrieved_docs.content: Because effort is significantly less in the absence of gravity, astronauts were given 2500 calories/day (instead of their normal 3000 Cal/day).

retrieved_docs.title: Radon Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Radon exposure is both an environmental and a health issue globally. Recent studies in 2012 have noted the highest levels of radon in Armenia, where it is estimated to have contributed to 29% to 30% of lung cancer cases. Japan had the lowest levels, with an estimated 4% of lung cancers attributed to radon. In the US, it is believed that 9% to 13% of lung cancer cases are caused by radon exposure. Historically, it was thought that radon exposure increases in regions where cold climate causes people to spend longer periods indoors. However, climate change and air conditioning have altered this pattern. High levels of radon exposure in a given region depend on the availability of radon in the environment and its ability to make its way into confined spaces/water sources, the ability of radon to concentrate in high levels, and the human behaviors that contribute to increased exposure for long periods.

retrieved_docs.title: CT Patient Safety And Care -- Issues of Concern -- Radiation Exposure with CT Scanning
retrieved_docs.content: There is no direct evidence of adverse biologic effects of radiation exposure in humans at doses less than 100 mSv. Most of the available evidence regarding adverse biologic effects of radiation exposure is derived from studies of survivors of atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Based on these survivors' studies, there is strong evidence of adverse biological effects, particularly an incremental increase in the incidence of various cancers at radiation doses exceeding 100 mSv. However, it is important to note that Hiroshima and Nagasaki survivors were instantaneously exposed to a mixture of whole-body radiation more complex than the low-energy X-ray beams used in conventional CT scanners. Another important note is that conventional CT scanners expose only one or a few organs to radiation. In contrast, the Japanese survivors of Hiroshima and Nagasaki were exposed to whole-body radiation. These factors complicate any derivation of accurate radiation risk to patients undergoing medical examination of CT scanning. Patients concerned about potential radiation risks from medical diagnostic imaging should consult with a radiologist or medical physicists to better understand risks versus benefits.

retrieved_docs.title: Ionizing Radiation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Cellphones, AM/FM radio, microwaves, sunbathing, and lighting are all nonionizing radiation sources. These are extremely low doses of ionizing radiation with no detectable health effects. However, long-term exposure may lead to cancer and other adverse health events.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiology, Image Production and Evaluation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Conventional radiography, fluoroscopy, and CT scan all use ionizing radiation to produce images, which is another issue of concern. Due to the rise in the use of CT scans and X-rays, it is important to note the harmful effects caused by radiation. Ionizing radiation causes detrimental biological effects on a cellular level either by indirectly producing free radicals to interrupt cellular metabolism or directly damaging the DNA of cells in the body. Cells are considered damaged when the level of cell damage exceeds the cell's ability to repair itself. There is an elevated risk of cancer development due to DNA mutations that occur with ionizing radiation. Studies of radiation-induced cancer from Hiroshima and Nagasaki have shown that there can be a 10 to 20 year latent period from the time an individual gets exposed to radiation to the time of cancer development. [9] Although the dose of radiation exposure from medical imaging tests is minuscule compared to the radiation from atomic bombs, excessive or unnecessary usage of these image modalities should be limited. All healthcare providers should be cautious and understand the risks associated with radiation. [10] [11] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Health Maintenance Wellness -- Issues of Concern -- Apollo
retrieved_docs.content: While deconditioning became obvious in space travelers returning from longer missions which included orbiting, landing on, and brief exploration of the moon, capsule size constraints precluded much in the way of counter-measures.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Physics -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer development is always an issue of concern when it comes to radiation exposure. In both internal and external radiation, ionizing radiation can damage DNA indirectly or directly. Direct damage typically occurs with high ionizing particles such as alpha or beta particles. These particles can interact and transfer their energy directly to the cell's DNA, causing single-stranded or double-stranded breaks or chromosomal damage. Radiation can also cause damage indirectly by the formation of free radicals produced through ionization of surrounding water. This mechanism is present in about two-thirds of all radiation-induced cell damage. It is always important to note the increased risk of cancer development due to DNA mutations that occur with ionizing radiation. [3] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: these compounds function as “allowed qualifiers” in the context of ionizing radiation because they can modify biological responses to radiation—either enhancing radiation‑induced damage, altering tissue radiosensitivity, or providing radioprotective or radiosensitizing effects. Awareness of these dual roles informs the integration of the agents into diagnostic imaging, radiotherapy planning, occupational radiation safety and broader radioprotection strategies, thereby optimizing therapeutic outcomes and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: In radiation biology, each qualifier can modulate, enhance, mitigate, or otherwise influence physiological responses to ionizing and non‑ionizing radiation. Hypoxanthine’s PARP‑inhibitory activity specifically suppresses DNA‑repair mechanisms, affecting cellular recovery after radiation‑induced damage. Recognizing these agents as radiation modifiers informs the design of protective strategies, therapeutic regimens, and risk assessments for both therapeutic radiation exposure and accidental radiation incidents. Collectively, the inclusion of these permissible qualifiers facilitates standardized manufacturing, regulatory compliance, targeted therapeutic development, and a comprehensive understanding of immunological, metabolic, and radiobiological processes, thereby improving patient care and advancing biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term used in radiotherapy to denote specific clinical conditions that are considered permissible or relevant when evaluating a patient for treatment. The allowed qualifiers for radiotherapy include respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest in adults, as well as عدوى بفيروس معوي (infection with an enteric virus). Recognizing these qualifiers helps clinicians assess patient suitability for radiotherapy, anticipate potential complications, and tailor therapeutic plans accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is considered a subdiscipline of radiation therapy, indicating that it falls under the broader field of radiation therapy, which encompasses the clinical use of ionizing radiation for treatment purposes, including both external beam radiotherapy and nuclear medicine techniques. This hierarchical relationship highlights that therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine are integral components of radiation therapy, contributing to the management of cancer and other conditions through targeted radiation-based interventions.

retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “Injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of aircraft” is linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). In turn, the tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). This relationship indicates that injuries resulting from an aircraft crash, even when the intent is unclear, may involve underlying tissue damage that can give rise to neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of evaluating both structural and neurotoxic consequences in the clinical assessment and management of such trauma.

Overall, the hierarchy connects general radioactive‑isotope examinations to specific imaging and therapeutic techniques: transmission imaging and myocardial imaging derive from the broad radioactive‑isotope examination; nuclear medicine diagnostic procedures branch into radionuclide localization of tumor and radioisotope studies of the central nervous system; radionuclide imaging actions serve as methods for these procedures; and therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine link diagnostic imaging to radiation‑based treatments such as radiation therapy, intracavitary radiotherapy, and stereotactic radiation surgery. This integrated framework clarifies how diagnostic nuclear‑medicine modalities and therapeutic radiation practices are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient evaluation and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is classified as a subcategory of الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة (examination using radioactive isotopes) and also falls under the broader domain of radiation therapy. Similarly, الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية (stereotactic radiosurgery) is identified as a subfield of الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة. These relationships illustrate that both therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine and stereotactic radiosurgery are linked to procedures that utilize radioactive isotopes, while therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine additionally aligns with the general practice of radiation therapy, highlighting the interconnected nature of diagnostic imaging and therapeutic radiation in modern medical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2108
retrieved_docs.content: Transmission imaging, therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine, and الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية (stereotactic radiosurgery) are all classified as sub‑domains of الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة (examination using radioactive isotopes). In addition, therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is placed under radiation therapy, reflecting its role in both diagnostic imaging with radioactive tracers and therapeutic use of ionizing radiation. This hierarchical arrangement underscores the close connection between diagnostic nuclear imaging techniques such as transmission imaging and therapeutic modalities, including stereotactic radiosurgery, all of which share a common foundation in the application of radioactive isotopes and radiation physics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2730
retrieved_docs.content: HIV, the anticoagulant dikumarol, the bile acid chenodeoxycholic acid, and various protective substances are all recognized as permissible qualifiers when describing the effects of radiation. This indicates that each of these factors can modify or influence radiation‑related outcomes, and therefore should be considered in the assessment of radiation effects. Incorporating infectious status (HIV), pharmacologic agents (dikumarol), specific metabolites (chenodeoxycholic acid), and protective agents into the evaluation of radiation effects enhances the accuracy of risk assessment and informs more tailored patient care and safety strategies.
